item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So many questions.

PMed, because respect for the absent. Those who have additional questions about this, please contact me or another staff member about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm always left feeling a little sad when I find a wonderful scip made by someone with a deleted account. What happened? Was he kicked out? Did he decide to leave? Why? So many questions.

Other than that, this piece right here is fantastic. Upvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the "anomalous average joe pissed off at everyone else for catching the Foundation's attention and ruining everything" conceit, and this uses it beautifully. I wonder how many of the other poignant and emotional things we have in containment are considered pretentious dingbats who need to get a job by their peers?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pig_Catapult should, he has the most recent edit and it says authorized by the author.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I…completely forgot who wrote this. Anyone remember?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At this time, SCP-1662-D pauses for approximately two minutes.

That's an ungodly long pause in a conversation.

That aside, I like. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Same here. That last bit, beautiful, definitely made this article for me!

+1 all the way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why such elaborate containment? It isn't mentioned that they are particularly strong or aggressive, rather they seem pasive and somewhat fragile. So why do they need specialized, armored, one-way glass cells arranged with tactics a forethought and their own permanently assigned riot control squad?

In the interview, Dr. Blackbox stops to transcribe. Why, and why right then? That seems like an unnecessary exposure to an essentially unknown anomaly. 1662-D's answers are already in writing, and I'd think a research scientist would have access to a tape recorder and a secretary or lab assistant to do his clerical work for him.

The table doesn't add much. It might be someto consider cutting, it is a rather long piece after all. -A has a pool cue for a foot? That would be incredibly awkward. His taped on foot is as long as he is tall.

Minor points all. I liked it, and voted accordingly. I like this idea of anomalous entities doing things, having lives even, beyond their anomalous nature.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They might not know for certain, but they could make hypotheses, or describe their observations of the occurance in greater detail, or something. I'm not saying you have to fill in all the blanks, but in the current article the unknowns feel like placeholders; they close off areas of interest rather than increasing interest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The problem with me filling these in is that they're things the Foundation can't actually exactly know. They can't know where they all are, or how many there are, because they've only ever caught four around the world, and only one has ever been willing to speak. If they have no method of making the three that actually have the ability submit to testing, then there's going to be a lot that they don't know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is left intentionally ambiguous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really don't like the number of unknowns in this article- they feel like very heavy handed stand-ins where they should advance the narrative. One might be alright- unknown number, or unspecified location, perhaps. And I understand that the foundation might not have an exact explanation for how it creates pigeons, or how those animals imprint on people, but to see presumably well trained scientists write down "We can't even guess how this stuff happens" so many times in a single article sounds almost like a parody.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1662 instances possess the ability to create, through an unknown means, various species of pigeon, rat, and squirrel native to the general area it occupies, as well as the ability to command and communicate with these animals

This is a bit ambiguous, as it might be taken to mean that it can control members of these species that it didn't create, not just the ones that it did create.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought it'd seem kinda silly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interview log was really what sealed the deal for me on this one. It just has a very good flow to it, and it sounds very natural, plus it neatly ties the concept of the article together. Nice one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Perhaps that should be mentioned.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1662-D: Can I ask my question now?

Dr. ███████: I can't promise I can answer it, but sure.

SCP-1662-D: I'm not going home, am I?

Dr. ███████: I'm afraid not.

+1 all the way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What wasn't mentioned was that 1662-D was also recovered wearing the most modern clothing: a simple black hoody and some jeans.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It comes off a bit long-winded, but I really like the twist of having one of them not be part of the group.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you. I'm really glad people like the interview.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Even the numbers are kinda similar (n^2)62. Good article, though. Like the interview.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, the rest is valid advice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …Huh, I hadn't thought of that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is it just me, or does this tie into SCP-962? Either way, upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upboated, but one small thing came to my attention.

the legitimacy of SCP-1662-D's testimonies are believed to be fabricated

Fabricated legitimacies and all that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted despite the weird sentence structures and occasional typos. Congratulations.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is one of my favorite themes among SCP-class containment articles. The idea of this group of odd mechanical entities existing, then being confused to their own existence, then manifesting the 'pauper's post' as a political idea among anomalous entities, rather than being innate in their nature, is awesome.

The world is odder still than we understand. There are things that live outside our understanding, and they have ideas, and factions, and political arrangements, and all sorts of things, beyond our little politics. Whatever made these metal men did not make them for this, no more than who ever made us fleshy men made us for empire or tyranny or whatever we do in our day to day lives. We exist, as they exist, and as both mayhaps always have and always will exist, and try to piece together meaning for why we do so. This is their attempt to find meaning.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, sorry, I guess I missed that.

I should sleep more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Regarding the last point:

Due to the nature of SCP-1662, all responses by SCP-1662-D are written, and all responses have been transcribed as they were written.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Due to the nature of SCP-1662, all responses by SCP-1662-D are written, and all responses have been transcribed as they were written.

This is my reply to your fourth pick.

EDIT: Derp, I cannot into Forums at 3:00 AM.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1662-A through -D are to be kept in separate 3m x 3m x 2.5m cells of 8.8cm thick transparent armor lined in one-way laminate to prevent the instances from observing one another while still allowing the constant monitoring from the outside.

This is a long sentence that can be shortened by removing "to prevent the instances from observing one another while still allowing the constant monitoring from the outside", because the quoted part is generally self-evident if you're using one-way laminate.

Instances display signs of sapience, and possess the ability to write in and understand various languages, which vary based mostly on the region the instance inhabits, as well as the knowledge of an at this time untranslated written code consisting of dots, various shapes, and the numbers zero (0) through seven (7), used presumably for communication between instances.

Really, really long sentences.

SCP-1662 will then follow the imprinted individual and deliver SCP-1662-1 personally, until the point that delivery is made, or the individual no longer fulfills the requirements

Deliver until the point that delivery is made? It seems a little redundant? Or maybe you should remove the last comma there, so it goes 'deliver it personally, until the point that delivery is made or the individual no longer fulfills the requirements' or something? This sentence was confusing to read.

or so the legend goes

I question how a person speaks in underlines as opposed to bold? Small and unsignificant point though.

Overall, I like it, but I don't understand that it is stated that they are unable to speak, but the -D guy speaks very well? Or am I doing something wrong? It feels a bit lengthened and overly complicated for what it is. +1 regardless, but I think it can do with some fat trimming.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What am I, chopped liver? :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you don't want them writing on them, give them an offline e-book reader. Honestly, it'd probably be cheaper than physical books anyway, and you know the researchers would dig the peek into their minds they'd get from the books they request.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The size of the cells was for emphasis on space: they don't have any muscles they need to stretch, and there's bound to be more than the four sometime in the future. As for the books, they'd likely be deprived of anything hands-on (especially books, considering that's something you could write on), but if it's a major issue I may consider revising this a bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: About the only observation I have is that I think the Foundation might treat these individuals better than they currently do. The Foundation is a prison, but it's not into cruel and unusual unless they have to, and locking individuals who are honestly pretty human in small boxes with a bed and a small amount of music probably qualifies as cruel and unusual. There are actively hostile humanoids who have better accommodations. I'm not saying let them wander freely, obviously, but possibly slightly larger cells, and expand good behavior rewards to include books and such?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One of my favorite parts of this is at the end of the interview, where what would be a generic complaint about the Foundation being a prison takes on a new depth because of the subject matter. I like it when an SCP strongly uses theme like that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The first SCP of the North American New Year! I hope it lives to the standards of being the first North American SCP of 2013.

This is something I've been working on for a while, and have desperately been trying to get to work. Thanks a lot to Silber, Sorts, and Voct for help with conceptual fixes and cleaning, and Echo for helping with the containment procedures.
